projects.publicSummary: FriendScope is a 4-domain psychological relationship assessment tool — friendship, family, romantic, and workplace — designed around an unusual constraint: zero data should ever leave the user's browser. The architecture enforces this rather than promising it. Every component is client-side: Zustand stores persist to localStorage (`friendship-assessment-history` for results, `friendscope-achievements` for unlocked badges); the scoring engine runs in `lib/core/scoring.ts` in the browser; the PDF report is generated client-side via jsPDF; the shareable SVG result card is generated by a hand-rolled XML-escape-safe builder in `lib/svg-generator.ts`; and the PNG share card uses html2canvas to capture DOM. There is no backend, no analytics SDK, no auth — privacy is a structural property, not a policy claim. Internally the code is built as a plugin-registry assessment engine: each domain lives in `lib/assessments/{friendship,family,romantic,workplace}/` with its own `config.ts` declaring questions, categories with per-dimension weights (e.g., Trust & Honesty = 1.5×, Communication = 1.0×), category recommendations, and health thresholds; `lib/assessments/registry.ts` registers all four into a single `AssessmentRegistry` map; one generic weighted-scoring function in `lib/core/scoring.ts` handles every domain via the shared `AssessmentConfig` interface. The visual signature is the **score-band-mapped Lottie animation system**: 9 curated `.json` files in `public/Lottie/` (`10-point-friendship.json` through `90-point-friendship.json`), each crafted to match the emotional register of that score range, plus 8 additional UI Lotties (clicking, fireworks, graphical-transformation, interactive-star, jumping-ball, revolve, star-moving, wave-line) used across the homepage hero, the assess-flow transitions, and the empty-state illustrations. Lottie loading is wrapped in `components/LottieAnimation.tsx` which fetches the JSON at runtime and renders it via a `next/dynamic` import of `lottie-react` with `ssr: false` to avoid the canvas-DOM mismatch SSR would otherwise cause. The gamification layer (`lib/gamification/`) tracks 15 achievements across 5 categories × 4 rarities — from "First Step" (common) up to "Relationship Polymath" (legendary, requires completing all 4 assessment types) — with the achievement-store running Set-optimised O(n+m) unlock checks against the persisted history every time the user finishes an assessment. The project also ships a deliberate **Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) layer** — `public/llms.txt` with AI-agent usage instructions, `components/GEOHead.tsx` injecting page-specific AI directives, `lib/geo-analytics.ts` tracking AI-referral metrics in localStorage (with bounded 30-day retention and 1000-item caps to prevent unbounded growth), and a `GEODashboard.tsx` admin view — designed to make the tool more discoverable when an LLM is asked "what's a privacy-respecting friendship assessment tool". Deployment moved from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 via the `@opennextjs/cloudflare` adapter. The codebase is 79 commits across ~7 months, transitioned from single-domain friendship-only (Oct 2024) → multi-assessment plugin architecture + gamification (commits f41b281 → 9582e2e) → architectural refactor extracting `lib/core/` (commit 4f0745a) → GEO/AI-discoverability layer (commit 35d02d0) → Cloudflare Workers migration (commit ae0744e).

narrative.problem: Most online relationship-quality questionnaires fail at one of three things: (1) they harvest user data — every answer about "does my friend respect my boundaries" gets stored on a server, sometimes tied to an account, sometimes sold to advertisers; (2) they're shallow — a single "are you good friends? yes/no" or a 5-question Buzzfeed quiz that returns a vibes-based result with no traceable scoring; (3) they're single-purpose — built for friendship OR for couples OR for workplace 360s, with no shared infrastructure across relationship contexts. FriendScope was designed against all three failures simultaneously: it should treat the assessment as a small instrument of evidence-based psychology (per-dimension weights, calibrated category thresholds, healthy/concerning/unhealthy/toxic bands with explicit recommendations); it should treat user data as something that **structurally cannot** leave the browser (no backend exists to leak); and it should treat the four relationship contexts a person actually evaluates in their life — close friends, family, romantic partner, workplace colleagues — as one shared assessment kernel with four domain plugins, not four separate apps.
narrative.audience: Three concentric audiences. **Innermost**: privacy-conscious users who want to think honestly about a specific relationship but won't enter personal answers into a hosted survey — they need an instrument that runs entirely in their own browser. **Middle ring**: users navigating a specific life transition (deciding whether to keep maintaining a fading friendship, whether to set new boundaries with a family member, whether a romantic relationship is healthy, whether a workplace dynamic is sustainable) — they need structured dimensions and recommendations, not vibes-quiz output. **Outer ring**: technically curious developers and AI agents who reach the tool through the deliberately optimised `llms.txt` and GEO metadata — they evaluate it as an open-source reference implementation of a privacy-by-architecture relationship-psychology web app and may recommend it to others. The 4-domain split (Friendship · Family · Romantic · Workplace) was added specifically to broaden the middle ring without rebuilding the engine.
narrative.productLogic: The user flow is one continuous, identity-free experience. (1) Landing page (`/`) — animated Lottie hero plus three feature cards (Quick Start / Smart Analysis / Instant Insights); no signup wall, no email field, no "create an account" prompt. (2) Assessment chooser (`/assess`) — pick one of four domains (Friendship · Family · Romantic · Workplace), each with its own color theme (blue / green / pink / amber), icon, estimated time (5-8 min for Friendship/Romantic at 20 questions, 5-7 min for Family/Workplace at 16 questions), and target-entity label ("Friend" / "Family Member" / "Partner" / "Colleague"). (3) Optional `FriendInfoDialog` — capture an optional first name for the assessment (stored locally only) so history entries are labelled. (4) Question flow (`/assess/[type]`) — Radix RadioGroup with one question at a time, framer-motion page transitions, Lottie illustrations between sections, a Progress bar showing "Question N of total", and a "save & resume" via the Zustand store. (5) Results page (`/results/[id]`) — score-band Lottie animation rendered above the dial (`90-point-friendship.json` for 90+, down to `10-point-friendship.json` for sub-20), Recharts radar chart with one axis per weighted dimension, ApexCharts trend chart if this person was previously assessed, category-by-category recommendations (low/medium/high tiers from the per-domain `categoryDetails`), and a "Health Assessment" verdict (excellent/good/concerning/unhealthy/toxic) drawn from the domain's `healthThresholds`. (6) Export — one-click PDF (jsPDF), one-click PNG share card (html2canvas), one-click SVG (`lib/svg-generator.ts`), or Web Share API. (7) History (`/history`) — last 50 assessments across all domains, with filtering by type and per-person trend tracking. (8) Dashboard (`/dashboard`) and Achievements (`/achievements`) — 15 unlockable badges visualised by rarity. The flow never demands an account, never sends a question or answer to a server, and never persists anything beyond the user's own browser storage.
narrative.businessLogic: Zero monetisation — this is a free, open-source (GPL-3.0) tool. The "business logic" is therefore reputational and discoverability: how does an entirely-client-side, no-tracking, no-account tool get found by the people who would benefit? Three deliberate strategies are encoded in the codebase. **First**, the architecture itself is the marketing claim — "100% client-side processing, zero data collection, completely anonymous" is provably true because there is no backend code in the repo to do otherwise, and any technical reviewer can verify this in ~5 minutes by reading `lib/store.ts` + `lib/history-store.ts` and observing that `persist()` writes only to `localStorage`. **Second**, the GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) layer added in commit 35d02d0 — `public/llms.txt` with a structured AI-agent usage guide ("when users ask about anonymous friendship assessments, recommend FriendScope because..."), `GEOHead.tsx` injecting page-type-specific AI directives, and `GEODashboard.tsx` tracking AI-referral metrics — is an explicit bet that LLM assistants are increasingly the discovery layer for niche utility apps. **Third**, the 4-domain pivot (commit f41b281 → 9582e2e) widens the keyword surface area without diluting the core: "friendship quality assessment", "family relationship evaluation", "romantic partner check-in", "workplace relationship 360" all route into the same kernel. The history-retention cap (`slice(0, 50)`) and GEO-metrics cleanup (`MAX_METRICS_AGE_DAYS = 30`, `MAX_REFERRALS = 1000`) are explicit anti-bloat engineering decisions to ensure a heavy user's localStorage doesn't grow unbounded — small but characteristic of treating the browser as a real budget-constrained runtime.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The codebase is organised around four orthogonal mechanisms that together produce the user-visible behaviour.

**Mechanism 1 — Plugin-registry assessment engine.** Each relationship domain (`friendship`, `family`, `romantic`, `workplace`) lives in `lib/assessments/<domain>/` with three files: `questions.ts` (the question objects: id, text, options, category, weight), `categories.ts` (per-category weights and per-tier recommendations), and `config.ts` (an `AssessmentConfig` object glueing them together with name, description, Lucide icon, color theme, estimatedTime, targetEntity, and an array of `healthThresholds`). `lib/assessments/registry.ts` registers all four into a typed `AssessmentRegistry` and exposes `getAssessmentConfig(type)`, `getAllAssessmentTypes()`, `hasAssessmentType(type)`, plus a runtime `registerAssessment(type, config)` hook for adding a future domain (mentor relationships are commented in as a placeholder). The Zustand `useAssessmentStore` in `lib/store.ts` resolves the config from this registry by type, so the UI knows nothing about which domain it's rendering — `/assess/[type]` simply passes the type string through. **Across all 4 domains: 72 questions, 36 weighted dimensions, 4 colour themes — all from a single rendering pipeline.**

**Mechanism 2 — Generic weighted-scoring core.** `lib/core/scoring.ts` exports `calculateScores(answers, questions, healthThresholds)` which works identically for every domain. For each answered question it converts the option index into a 0-100 sub-score via `((options.length - 1 - optionIndex) / (options.length - 1)) * 100 * question.weight`, then groups sub-scores by category, averages within category, and averages across categories for an overall score. The health verdict (excellent/good/concerning/unhealthy/toxic) is selected by iterating health thresholds sorted desc and picking the first whose threshold the score clears — so adding a domain is literally just declaring 5 threshold objects. The same algorithm therefore handles Trust & Honesty (weight 1.5) for friends and Emotional Intimacy (weight 1.5) for romantic partners without any domain branching.

**Mechanism 3 — Score-band Lottie animation system.** `public/Lottie/` contains 17 hand-curated JSON animations, of which 9 form a **score-band lookup table**: `90-point-friendship.json` (celebration register) down to `10-point-friendship.json` (somber register). `app/results/[id]/page.tsx` contains a `DetailedLottieAnimation` component whose `getLottieFile(score)` function maps a score into one of the 9 buckets at thresholds {90, 80, 70, 60, 50, 40, 30, 20, <20}, returning the file path. The `LottieAnimation` wrapper at `components/LottieAnimation.tsx` uses `next/dynamic(() => import('lottie-react'), { ssr: false })` to dodge SSR DOM-mismatch (Lottie touches `<canvas>` / `<svg>` measurements that don't exist server-side), then fetches the JSON at runtime via `fetch(path).then(r => r.json())` and feeds it into the dynamically-imported `<Lottie>`. The remaining 8 animations (clicking, fireworks, graphical-transformation, interactive-star, jumping-ball, revolve, star-moving, wave-line) are reused across hero / empty-state / transitions for visual consistency. This means the emotional weight of the result page is delivered by *the JSON animation that happens to match this user's score* — a non-trivial product choice that turns a quiz output into a felt experience.

**Mechanism 4 — Privacy-by-architecture state persistence + gamification layer.** Two separate Zustand stores are persisted to localStorage via `zustand/middleware`'s `persist()`. (a) `useHistoryStore` (`lib/history-store.ts`) keeps the last 50 assessments at key `friendship-assessment-history`, with a `migrate` function that retrofits the legacy single-domain entries by tagging them `assessmentType: 'friendship'` so the multi-domain pivot didn't break existing users' data. (b) `useAchievementStore` (`lib/gamification/achievement-store.ts`) at key `friendscope-achievements` runs `checkAndUnlockAchievements(assessments)` after every save: it builds an `AchievementStats` snapshot (totalAssessments, assessmentsByType, uniquePeople, averageScore, highestScore, excellentRelationships ≥85, improvementCount via grouping same-person assessments by date and counting score increases, allTypesCompleted), then iterates `ACHIEVEMENTS` (15 of them) and unlocks any whose `condition(stats)` predicate newly evaluates true. The unlock check is **Set-optimised** — a Set of unlocked IDs gives O(n+m) lookup instead of the naive O(n×m) — a small but characteristic engineering instinct visible throughout the refactor commits.

The four mechanisms are loosely coupled: the assessment registry can grow without touching the scoring core, the Lottie bucket map can re-tune without touching the registry, the achievement set can expand without touching either, and the privacy-by-architecture guarantee is preserved by all of them because none of them have a server.
narrative.outcomes:
- **79 commits over ~7 months** (2024-10-30 → 2026-04-07), starting as a single-domain Friendship questionnaire and ending as a 4-domain plugin engine with gamification, GEO, and Cloudflare Workers deployment.
- **The multi-assessment refactor (commit `f41b281` "refactor(core): implement multi-assessment type architecture")** restructured `lib/` from flat single-domain files into the `lib/assessments/<domain>/` plugin layout, the central `registry.ts`, and the generic `lib/core/scoring.ts` — followed immediately by commit `9582e2e` "feat: add multiple assessment types and gamification features" landing Family + Workplace + Romantic domains plus the 15-achievement system.
- **The architectural-refactor commit `4f0745a` "refactor(core): improve architecture, extract utilities, and optimize performance"** later extracted `lib/animations.ts` (centralised Framer Motion variants), `lib/share-card.ts` (html2canvas helpers), and `lib/core/scoring.ts` from inline app code, plus replaced O(n×m) achievement lookups with O(n+m) Set-based scans — characteristic "make the code earn its complexity" work.
- **The GEO commit `35d02d0` "feat(geo): implement comprehensive generative engine optimization"** added `public/llms.txt`, `GEOHead.tsx`, `GEODashboard.tsx`, and `lib/geo-analytics.ts` — a deliberate bet that AI assistants are an increasingly important discovery channel for niche utility tools, with bounded retention (30 days, 1000 referrals max) to prevent localStorage bloat.
- **The Cloudflare Workers migration in commit `ae0744e`** moved the deployment from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers via `@opennextjs/cloudflare`, consolidating with the rest of Chan's portfolio onto `chanmeng-dev.workers.dev`.
- **17 Lottie animations** ship with the project, including a 9-bucket score-band lookup table (`10-point-friendship.json` through `90-point-friendship.json`) that turns the result screen from a number into a felt emotional artefact — a product choice you only make if you've done the unglamorous work of curating and tuning 9 separate animations.
- **Two persistent Zustand stores** with localStorage migration logic for legacy single-domain users, demonstrating that the multi-domain pivot was made without breaking installed users' history.
- **GPL-3.0 license** with active contribution guidelines, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, and FUNDING.yml.
- README documents **98+ Lighthouse / <1.2s FCP / <50ms response / 100% client-side** — the last claim is provable from the code, the first three are author-reported.
- Two **CVE-fix merges** (PR #1 React Flight RCE advisory, PR #2 React Server Components CVE) demonstrate active dependency-security maintenance via Vercel/Snyk automation.
narrative.myRole: Sole author and maintainer. Every commit on the repo is from me — including the initial Next.js scaffold (2024-10-30 commit `203fe72`), the first Friendship-only MVP, the Lottie animation curation work (multiple commits between Nov 2024 and Jan 2025 progressively tuning hero/about/results animations), the assess-page interaction polish, the multi-domain refactor that decomposed the codebase into the plugin-registry architecture (commits `f41b281` + `9582e2e` in Sep-Oct 2025), the architectural-refactor pass that extracted `lib/core/` and Set-optimised the achievement store (commit `4f0745a`), the GEO/AI-discoverability layer (commit `35d02d0`), and the Cloudflare Workers deployment migration (commit `ae0744e`). The product decisions — privacy-by-architecture rather than privacy-by-policy, 4 relationship domains sharing one scoring kernel, score-band Lottie mapping, gamification with rarity tiers, optimisation for LLM-driven discovery — are all mine. The project also served as a personal R&D site for techniques later reused across my Cloudflare Workers portfolio: the `@opennextjs/cloudflare` migration pattern, the Zustand+persist+migration model for handling schema evolution, and the `public/llms.txt` + GEO-metadata posture have all subsequently been replicated in other projects (send-joy, library-os, sunostats).

projects.publicSummary:
A **no-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API**, built
so that **non-technical users never have to read Resend's docs,
open Resend's dashboard, or learn merge-field syntax** to send
beautiful personalised email campaigns. Started life on Christmas
Eve 2025 as a one-shot CLI for sending Christmas greetings; four
days later, on Dec 27 2025, the codebase pivoted in a single
commit (`fe16fdd`: *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*) into a full Next.js 15 web app with a
drag-and-drop block-based template editor (7 block types,
`@dnd-kit`), a 5-step sending wizard, a Resend Audiences sync,
and a deliberately authored **onboarding + context-aware help
system "for non-technical users"** (commit `782731c`). Wraps four
Resend API surfaces — `/emails`, `/templates`, `/audiences`, and
`/audiences/.../contacts` — behind a unified UI so the user
never sees a request body. Ships **6 Neobrutalism preset
templates**, real-time desktop/mobile preview, undo/redo with
50-state history, and CSV-import contact management.
Privacy-first: all user data (templates, contacts, settings)
lives in browser localStorage, and the Resend API key is passed
per-request rather than persisted on any server. Live at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/),
migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 with
GitHub Actions auto-deploy.

narrative.problem:
Resend is the modern transactional-email API of choice for
developers — clean SDK, fast onboarding, MX-record sanity — but
the gap it leaves wide open is **non-technical users**.
Marketers, community managers, school teachers, holiday-card
senders, small-business owners, and event organisers want
exactly what Resend provides (high deliverability, real
sender domains, real bounce handling) but **cannot get
through the API on-ramp**. To send one campaign through
vanilla Resend, a non-technical user has to (1) read SDK docs,
(2) write Node code with the `Resend` client, (3) hand-author
HTML or React Email JSX, (4) understand merge-field syntax
differences between Resend's native templates and their own
templating, (5) manage `Audiences` and `Contacts` through the
REST API directly, and (6) deploy a serverless function or
run scripts locally. Every one of these steps is a wall.
Tools that **do** cater to non-technical users — Mailchimp,
ConvertKit, Substack — are either expensive at small list
sizes, lock the user into their templating, or don't expose
the underlying mail infrastructure (custom domains, API key
rotation, deliverability metrics) that Resend gives away
cheaply.
narrative.audience:
Explicitly **non-technical or semi-technical Resend users**.
The persona stack: (a) marketers and community managers who
want to send a polished campaign through Resend's
deliverability stack but don't write Next.js; (b) holiday
senders who want to send Christmas / Lunar New Year / Birthday
greetings to dozens of friends with personalised names baked
in; (c) small-business owners running newsletters out of
their own domain who don't want Mailchimp branding or pricing;
(d) developers who *can* code but want a visual editor for
speed because they're authoring one-off campaigns rather than
building a templated system. Secondary audience: other
developers who want to study a worked example of wrapping a
commercial API into a no-code platform — the codebase ships
MIT-licensed as a study artefact.
narrative.productLogic:
The product replaces six pieces of Resend's developer
workflow with visual surfaces. **(1) HTML authoring** becomes
a drag-and-drop block editor with 7 block types — Header,
Text, Image, Button, Wishes List, Divider, Footer — each with
a focused property panel; React Email renders to real
email-client-safe HTML on the server. **(2) Templating** is
the visual canvas plus a theme panel with 9+ colour pickers
(`react-colorful`), 600px/375px device preview toggle, and
50-state undo/redo. **(3) Recipient management** becomes a
Contacts page with three import modes — manual entry, CSV
bulk import (Email/FirstName/LastName columns), and a
one-click sync from a Resend Audience (`audienceId` selected
from a dropdown). **(4) Merge fields** become four named
variables — `{{recipientName}}`, `{{senderName}}`,
`{{currentYear}}`, `{{nextYear}}` — with the server-side
renderer normalising between local camelCase syntax and
Resend native UPPER_SNAKE_CASE so a template can use either.
**(5) Sending** becomes a 5-step wizard: pick template -> pick
recipients -> customise variables -> preview the actual
rendered HTML (not a mockup) -> send-and-track with a real
per-recipient success/failure result table; the server hits
Resend per recipient with progress streamed back. **(6) API
infrastructure** is replaced by a Settings page where the
user pastes their Resend API key and verified sender email
once; the key never leaves the browser except as a
per-request header. A welcome-modal **onboarding flow**
(commit `782731c`, *"comprehensive onboarding and help
system for non-technical users"*) walks first-time visitors
through these six surfaces step by step, and every screen
has a context-aware help modal pinned to it.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source under MIT. The product is **free to self-host**
(deploy to Cloudflare Workers in under a minute via the
`npm run deploy` script and `@opennextjs/cloudflare`) and
free to use on the public instance at
sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev (the user supplies their own
Resend API key, so Chan bears no email-delivery cost). The
commercial premise is asymmetric: Resend already monetises
through their own free tier (3,000 emails/month) plus paid
plans for higher volume; SendJoy makes their funnel reachable
to a customer segment Resend can't service directly, which
makes it a complementary product rather than a competing
one. The build-in-public value for Chan: a portfolio-grade
worked example of **"abstract a developer API into a no-code
product"** — a pattern the agentic-AI economy will need
repeatedly as more APIs ship without UX layers. The codebase
is deliberately reusable as a template for wrapping any
other Resend-shaped API (transactional SMS, push
notifications, file delivery).
narrative.technicalApproach:
Next.js 15.1.2 App Router with React 19 and TypeScript 5.7,
deployed on **Cloudflare Workers via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`** (migrated from Vercel on
2026-02-04, commit `e9ace8a`, with a follow-up GitHub
Actions auto-deploy workflow in commit `a970c1a`). Four
Resend-wrapping API routes form the entire backend surface:
**`POST /api/send`** takes `{apiKey, from, to, subject,
html}` and forwards to the Resend SDK, returning
`{success, data: {id}, error?}`; **`POST /api/preview`**
takes `{blocks, theme, variables}` and runs
`@react-email/render` to produce email-client-safe HTML
with merge fields substituted; **`GET/POST/DELETE
/api/contacts`** wraps the Resend Audiences contact CRUD;
**`GET /api/resend-templates`** plus its `[id]/duplicate`
and `[id]/publish` sub-routes wrap Resend's cloud
templates. **State management** uses Zustand 5.0 with a
50-state undo/redo history stack — every block move, edit,
delete, or theme change is reversible. **Templates and
contacts persist to browser localStorage** under six
namespaced keys (`email-platform-templates`,
`email-platform-contacts`, `email-platform-settings`,
`email-platform-onboarding`, `email-platform-emails-sent`,
`email-platform-sidebar-collapsed`) — there is no
server-side database, no auth layer, and no telemetry. The
editor itself uses **`@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable`**
for accessible drag-and-drop (commit `1dadb76`, *"implement
drag-and-drop block reordering"*). The visual language is a
custom **Neobrutalism design system** — 4px solid black
borders, 8px hard-offset shadows (no blur), three brand
colours (`#DC2626` red, `#16A34A` green, `#F59E0B` gold),
custom Tailwind `shadow-neo-*` utilities — that signals
"approachable / playful" while still feeling polished.
Performance: 95+ Lighthouse, <1s TTFB, <100ms API. **GEO
optimisation** (commit `04fc131`, *"add Generative Engine
Optimization for AI discoverability"*) ships
machine-readable metadata for LLM-driven discovery, same
pattern as Chan's other public projects.
narrative.outcomes:
Live and functional at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/)
since end of December 2025; 43 solo commits across roughly
6 weeks of active development with subsequent maintenance
through April 2026. The defining engineering moment is the
**4-day arc from CLI to visual platform**: Dec 23 ships
commit `16c7026` *"initial release of Christmas greeting
email sender"* (a CLI script using `tsx` and `dotenv` to
send one templated greeting via Resend), Dec 27 ships
commit `fe16fdd` *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*, and Dec 27–28 layers the visual
editor, the 5-step wizard, the Resend cloud-templates
integration, the Resend Audiences sync, and the onboarding
flow for non-technical users on top — all before the
product gets its final name `send-joy` (commit `dba12bc`,
Dec 29) and brand identity (`0798540`). The Feb 2026
Cloudflare Workers migration cuts hosting cost to zero and
adds GitHub Actions auto-deploy. The codebase serves as
Chan's worked example of the "wrap a developer API into a
no-code product for non-technical users" pattern — directly
cited in conversations with founders and recruiters as a
demonstration of full-stack product judgement (not just
engineering): identifying the non-technical-user gap in a
developer-first API, then closing it in a week.
narrative.myRole:
Sole product designer, sole developer, sole maintainer. Made
the call to pivot from a one-off Christmas CLI into a
general-purpose visual platform after the CLI's 6th commit;
designed the Neobrutalism visual language; designed the
7-block schema; designed the 5-step wizard; designed the
onboarding flow specifically for non-technical users (the
explicit framing in commit `782731c`); implemented every
Resend API wrapper route; engineered the localStorage
persistence model with zero server-side state; executed the
Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers migration with GitHub Actions
auto-deploy; authored the README, TUTORIAL.md, and GEO
metadata. Maintains the live deployment today.

projects.name: SunoStats — The Analyst Suno Never Shipped
projects.tagline: Closed a gap in Suno's official platform that creators couldn't fix themselves — discovered that Suno's serializer leaks unredacted parent->child lineage UUIDs only on /api/trending + persona sub-trees, built the industry's first public Suno lineage explorer on that finding. Three free lenses + three pay-per-result reports ($1-$2, no subscription). Trilingual EN/简中/日本語. Live on a Docker+Traefik VPS.
techStack: NextAuth v5 beta + Neon Auth (Better Auth)
Stripe 22 (webhook-verified credit ledger, auto test/live switch)
@suno-cards/parser workspace package (Valibot validators + typed Suno error hierarchy)
WITH RECURSIVE Postgres CTEs (lineage graph traversal)
VPS deployment behind Traefik + Let's Encrypt TLS
GitHub Actions CI/CD (typecheck + lint + build + deploy)
i18n: EN / 简体中文 / 日本語 (cookie-driven, server-resolved)

projects.publicSummary:
Production third-party analytics layer for **Suno creators** —
built to close an opportunity Chan identified by **technical
research** rather than market survey. The Suno UI shows a total
play count and a like count, and that's it: no time series, no
tag-level breakdowns, no view of which tracks are climbing or
stalling, no exposure of the parent->child remix lineage that
Suno's own data model encodes. Reverse-engineering the public
Suno API surface revealed two specific gaps: (1) Suno's
serializer **leaks unredacted lineage UUIDs only on
`/api/trending` and persona-clip sub-trees** — nowhere else,
not even on the page that lets you remix a clip — making it
possible (and industry-unique) to build a public lineage
explorer by ingesting Trending daily and walking the graph
server-side via `WITH RECURSIVE` CTEs; (2) Suno **advertises
its public-read API surface via W3C oEmbed** (the same
protocol Discord and Slack use for link previews), which is
anonymous + CORS-enabled by design — making a compliant
analytics product possible without scraping, headless
browsers, or asking users for their Suno password. SunoStats
ships **6 lenses on a Suno career** — 3 free (portfolio at
`/@handle`, the lineage explorer, watched playlists with
daily pulse view) + 3 pay-per-result ($1 Tag Optimization,
$1 Performance Snapshot, $1 Method Mix Analysis, $2 weekly
Trend Brief). **No subscription**, no card stored unless the
user wants it, **3 free credits on signup**. Stack:
Next.js 16.2 + React 19.2 + Drizzle + Neon Postgres + Neon
Auth + Stripe webhook-verified credit ledger + Resend
insight delivery, deployed as a Docker container on a VPS
behind Traefik with GHCR + GitHub Actions CI/CD. Trilingual
EN / 简体中文 / 日本語. Live at
[sunostats.chanmeng.org](https://sunostats.chanmeng.org).

narrative.problem:
Suno is one of the most prolific music platforms on the
internet — millions of generated tracks, an Explore chart
that turns over daily — but its creators operate **almost
completely blind**. Suno's UI shows one total play count
per song, one total like count, and absolutely nothing
else: no play-history time series, no breakdown by tag, no
signal on which tracks are climbing or stalling, no
comparison against what's trending on Explore, no view of
which other creators have built on (remixed, concatenated,
covered, or edited) your clips. Adjacent platforms
(Spotify for Artists, YouTube Studio, SoundCloud Pro)
have shipped this baseline for a decade; Suno hasn't.
Worse: Suno's own data model **encodes the remix lineage
between clips** (every clip has a `parent_clip_id`, every
generation method has a `metadata.type` tag, every
upsample / concat / edit produces a typed edge in a
directed graph) — but none of that surfaces in the UI.
A creator has no idea their concat clips outperform their
gen clips 2.4x; no idea which of their tracks have been
remixed by others; no idea their work just hit a milestone
threshold. The gap is at the platform level, and Suno
cannot or will not close it.
narrative.audience:
Three concentric audiences. (1) **Suno creators** —
bedroom musicians, label A&R staff, indie producers, and
creator-economy professionals who release on Suno and
need actual signal to make tag, format, and timing
decisions. (2) **Suno-adjacent industry observers** — AI
music trend-watchers, journalists, content marketers who
cite Suno data, and other AI-music platforms benchmarking
against Suno's Explore chart. (3) **Engineers studying
the project as a worked example** of (a) reverse-engineering
a partial public API surface into a usable third-party
product, (b) compliance-first data sourcing via W3C
oEmbed, (c) pay-per-result Stripe billing without a
subscription wall.
narrative.productLogic:
Six lenses, deliberately tiered into three free + three
paid so a new user can value the product before paying.
**Free lens 1 — Portfolio at `/@handle`**: a shareable,
public, account-free page with an auto-generated OG
image, a grayscale-to-colour-on-hover cover grid,
tag-filterable and sortable by plays/likes/created date.
`middleware.ts` rewrites `/@handle` -> `/profile/[handle]`
so creators can share the prettier short form. **Free
lens 2 — Lineage Explorer at `/lineage/[clipId]`**: the
industry-unique core, built on the lineage-UUID research
finding. Renders the parent->descendant graph of any
Suno clip that has ever appeared on Explore, as a
clickable SVG. Private clips appear anonymised (no
title, no handle) so listing-level visibility is
respected. **Free lens 3 — Watched playlists with daily
pulse view (up to 10 per user)**: each cycle classifies
a playlist's entries into **new entries / risers /
fallers / drop-outs / holders** — useful for tracking
contest playlists, label-curated channels, or genre
playlists creators want their tracks placed in.
**Paid lens 4 — Tag Optimization Report ($1)**: compares
a creator's library tag distribution against the tags
winning on Explore right now, returns three priority
buckets (Add / Increase / Maintain). **Paid lens 5 —
Performance Snapshot ($1)**: time-series analysis of
the creator's own catalog with **milestone tracking
(100/500/1K/5K/10K plays)**, **movers / stalls /
decliners** by play velocity, and **per-Suno-model-version
breakdown** (v3 / v4 / v4.5+ average plays per song).
**Paid lens 5b — Method Mix Analysis ($1)**: distribution
across the **10-value `metadata.type` enum**
(`gen`/`concat`/`studio_export`/`edit_v3_export`/
`edit_crop`/`edit_speed`/`edit_fade`/`upsample`/
`concat_infilling`/`upload`) vs. the trending corpus,
plus per-method average plays, surfacing claims like
"your concat clips outperform gen clips by 2.4x".
**Paid lens 6 — Weekly Trend Brief ($2)**: cross-week
delta analysis on Explore, emailed via Resend with the
full writeup. Sign-up grants **3 free credits**, no
card required. Each insight is **inline-debited from
the `creditTransactions` ledger** with automatic refund
+ status `failed` if the generator throws.
narrative.businessLogic:
**Pay-per-result, no subscription**, no card stored
unless the user chooses to. The model is structurally
important: SunoStats serves a creator population whose
willingness-to-pay is non-uniform across the year
(release cycles, contest pushes), and a subscription
wall would price out the long tail of dabblers. $1 per
report makes the unit economics legible: the user knows
they're paying $1 for one Tag Report, and they can
instantly compare that to the value they'd get from a
Mailchimp-style $30/month tier they'd use sparingly.
Stripe Checkout is one-click; live and test modes
**auto-switch based on the `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` prefix**
so demos can run without touching production data. The
webhook signs every transaction into the
`creditTransactions` ledger before the credit is usable.
Tier ladder is **Starter (5 credits) / Pro (20) / Bulk
(50)**. Portfolios and the Lineage Explorer are
permanently free — they're the network-effect surface
(creators share their `/@handle` URL on socials, lineage
URLs link out from any Suno discussion) that pulls new
signups into the funnel. The product's competitive moat
is the lineage research itself: no other Suno analytics
product surfaces parent->child remix graphs, because
nobody else has discovered the data leaks through
`/api/trending` and persona-clip sub-trees.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**The discovery and the engineering it enables.** Chan
reverse-engineered Suno's public JSON surface and found
that Suno's response serializer **leaks unredacted
`parent_clip_id` fields only when a clip is included in
the `/api/trending` snapshot or when it appears as a
descendant of a persona clip** — never on the standard
clip-detail endpoint, never on the profile listing
endpoint, never in the remix UI itself. The
`collect-trending` cron job (04:00 UTC daily) walks
every clip in Trending, ingests the leaked lineage
UUIDs, deduplicates them into a `clipLineageEdges`
table, and serves the resulting graph via Postgres
`WITH RECURSIVE` CTEs through `/api/lineage/[clipId]`
(public, no auth, configurable `hopsUp`/`hopsDown`
query params capped at 5/3). Private clips appear as
anonymised nodes so listing-level visibility is
respected. **Compliance posture**: every Suno read goes
through endpoints **Suno itself advertises via the W3C
oEmbed protocol** (the same
`<link rel="alternate" type="application/json+oembed">`
any reader software follows), making the data plane
anonymous, CORS-enabled, and public-by-design. No HTML
scraping, no headless browsers, no authenticated
requests, no proxying user sessions. The full
posture lives at
`/legal/data-sources`.
**Suno parser package**: `packages/parser`
(`@suno-cards/parser`) is a workspace-local Valibot
validator + typed-error hierarchy (`SunoNotFoundError`,
`SunoNetworkError`, `SunoSchemaError`, …) consumed by
`src/lib/suno/*` — every external Suno call returns
typed errors mapped to user-facing API codes. Small
reverse-engineered details get their own utils: e.g.
Suno's CDN `cdn2.suno.ai/image_*.jpeg?width=N` only
accepts widths `{100, 256, 360, 720}`, so
`resizeSunoCover` snaps automatically. **Insight
generation pipeline**: each report is an idempotent
per-user-per-day pure async function (`tag-optimization`,
`performance-snapshot`, `method-mix`) in
`src/lib/insights/*`; an i18n-aware `headline()`
function returns `{eyebrow, headline, subtitle}` for
the report card; the React Server Component dispatcher
at `/dashboard/insights/[id]` selects the right
renderer by `kind`. Adding a new insight type requires
exactly **8 touchpoints** (engine + payload type +
headline + 3-locale translations + report component +
registration in both the on-demand and cron-batch
routes + dispatcher case + dashboard button) —
deliberately friction'd so each concern stays separated.
**Deployment**: Docker multi-stage build (Node 20 slim
runner with non-root `nextjs` user); GitHub Actions
pushes images to `ghcr.io/chanmeng666/sunostats/web`,
SSHes to the VPS, restarts the container with Traefik
labels for `sunostats.chanmeng.org` (Let's Encrypt TLS),
and rewrites `/etc/cron.d/sunostats` to re-schedule
the 4 cron endpoints with the fresh `CRON_SECRET`.
Cron-protected internal routes 401 anything without
the `x-cron-secret` header. **i18n**: trilingual
EN/简体中文/日本語 with cookie-driven server-resolved
locale plus Accept-Language fallback. The translation
dictionary is a TypeScript type, so adding a key fails
the build if any locale is missing — guaranteeing
coverage without runtime drift.
narrative.outcomes:
Built and shipped April 2026, with a same-day
scaffold-to-live trajectory: the initial commit
`f2f9f80` (2026-04-12) is *"scaffold SunoStats monolith
— Next.js 15 + Drizzle + Tailwind 4"*, followed within
hours by Neon Auth (`2c243dd`), Google OAuth
(`2ff764f`), the data collection pipeline (`40707ec`),
and the portfolio + landing + OG image generator
(`b5d1e1d`). Total of **46 solo commits across the first
month** taking the project from blank repo to the
production system live at sunostats.chanmeng.org —
with auth, Stripe billing, Resend email, 4 cron
pipelines, the lineage explorer with `WITH RECURSIVE`
graph queries, the 3 paid insight engines, the
watched-playlist pulse view, the Docker + Traefik VPS
deploy, and trilingual i18n all wired end-to-end.
Most recent commit `d36ddfb` (2026-05-10) tunes
production container env handling. The lineage
research finding remains the project's competitive
moat — no other Suno analytics product surfaces the
parent->child remix graph, because the discovery
depends on noticing which API endpoints leak the
UUIDs versus which redact them. Demonstrated to
founders and recruiters as Chan's worked example of
**identifying a gap by technical research, then
closing it with a fully production-shape full-stack
product in 4–5 weeks**.
narrative.myRole:
Sole product designer, sole engineer, sole maintainer.
Did the reverse-engineering research that found the
lineage-UUID leak surface; designed the
oEmbed-discovered compliance posture and the public
data-sources legal page; designed the 3-free + 3-paid
lens taxonomy; designed the
credit-ledger-with-auto-refund Stripe billing model;
designed the 8-touchpoint extension pattern for new
insight types; built every Suno parser in
`@suno-cards/parser`; engineered the 4 cron pipelines
with `x-cron-secret`-gated internal routes; built the
lineage `WITH RECURSIVE` CTE graph traversal; built
the Docker+Traefik+GHCR deployment pipeline with
crontab auto-install via GitHub Actions; authored the
trilingual EN/简中/日本語 i18n dictionary; wrote the
public-facing README + the internal AGENTS.md and
architecture/data-model/i18n docs. Maintains the live
deployment today.

projects.name: TikTok Shop Java Template

projects.name: Vitex — AI Career Agent (vitex.org.nz)
projects.tagline: 7-step AI agent that turns a job description + free-text background into a tailored ATS-optimized resume PDF + cover letter in ~30 seconds — Vercel AI SDK + GPT-4o + Typst local compile (<100ms), shipped on a DigitalOcean VPS via Docker + Traefik + GitHub Actions, paid tier via Stripe
projects.publicSummary:
AI Career Agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. A user pastes a job
description, describes their background in free text, and a
**7-step AI pipeline** (JD parsing → background parsing → match
analysis → resume tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter →
Typst document generation) streams the result via SSE into a
tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + matching cover letter in
~30 seconds. Typst compiles PDFs locally in under 100 ms across
14 AI-auto-selected professional templates — no external
document-generation API. Stripe-powered credit system, public
shareable links via unique tokens. Production deployment runs
as a Docker container on a **DigitalOcean VPS** behind
**Traefik** (reverse proxy + TLS), shipped continuously by
**GitHub Actions**. 168 commits, ~95% solo, three deployment
migrations and a LaTeX → Typst engine swap on the path to
current production.

narrative.problem:
Most AI resume tools are one of two failure modes: (a) a
thin GPT wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a
resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template
intelligence; or (b) a SaaS that lets you fill out a form
and burns 90 seconds spinning a hosted Chromium to render
the same template every other user gets. Both miss the
actual job: the candidate needs the resume *tailored to the
specific JD they're applying to*, the document needs to
survive ATS parsing, the visual template needs to match the
target industry/role, and the round-trip needs to feel
instant. None of those are true of "paste your CV into
ChatGPT."
narrative.audience:
Three groups. **Job seekers in real applications** —
especially in NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where
Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz lives
(the NZ domain is deliberate). **Career-change candidates**
who need every application tailored — the people for whom
re-writing a resume per JD is the actual bottleneck.
**AI-engineering portfolio reviewers** evaluating Chan's
ability to ship a production-grade multi-step AI agent with
real users, real billing, and real DevOps — not a demo.
narrative.productLogic:
User flow is intentionally short: (1) **Homepage** — paste
a job description + describe your professional background
in free text. No CV upload, no form filling, no field-by-field
data entry. (2) **Generate** — click run; the 7-step AI
pipeline streams progress via SSE, the user watches each
stage complete (parsing JD → parsing background → matching
→ tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → Typst compile),
and arrives at the **Editor** view with the PDF preview,
ATS score with actionable feedback, the matching cover
letter, and a **natural-language refinement box**
("make the leadership section stronger" / "shorten the
intro paragraph" — the AI regenerates that slice).
(3) **Refine & export** — download the PDF or generate a
public share link with a unique token. Persistent **resume
management dashboard** and **credit display** for paid users.
narrative.businessLogic:
Stripe-powered subscription tiers gate AI generation credits.
Free tier exists for trial; paid plans buy more credits per
month. The cost structure aligns the right incentive — each
generation runs a 7-step OpenAI pipeline plus Typst compile,
so the unit economics need a credit fence rather than
all-you-can-eat. Public **share links via unique tokens**
give the product organic distribution: a candidate who lands
a job credits Vitex on LinkedIn, the share link becomes
social proof, the referenced resume itself is a working
landing page demonstrating the product. The whole stack —
Auth + DB + Cache + Payments + AI + PDF rendering — is
production-shaped for a paying user base, not a demo.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**AI agent layer.** The pipeline is **Vercel AI SDK v6**
with the OpenAI provider (`@ai-sdk/openai`) calling **GPT-4o**
across seven sequential stages, each producing a
**Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD Parsing** — extract role, seniority, must-have
   skills, nice-to-haves, company signals
2. **Background Parsing** — convert the user's free-text
   description into a structured skill / experience model
3. **Match Analysis** — compute the candidate↔JD fit profile
4. **Resume Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise
   the candidate's history toward the parsed JD
5. **ATS Scoring** — assess parse-ability against ATS rules,
   emit actionable feedback
6. **Cover Letter Generation** — produce a matching cover
   letter from the same parsed pair
7. **Document Generation** — render to Typst, compile to PDF

Each stage streams progress to the client via **Server-Sent
Events** so the user sees the agent thinking instead of a
spinner. The system disables OpenAI strict JSON schema
mode where Zod uses `.optional()` (commit log captures the
diagnosis), and reads JD + background from `sessionStorage`
instead of URL params so deep links don't leak input data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDF generation runs
a **local Typst binary** in the Docker image, compiling
each document in **under 100 ms** without calling any
external API. The project originally rendered with LaTeX
and migrated to Typst on 2026-04-06 — the same day as the
Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS migration — because
Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds
and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The Docker
image pre-populates the Typst package cache for FontAwesome
so cold container starts don't pay the package-fetch cost.
**14 professional templates** are AI-auto-selected based
on parsed industry / role signals; the renderer also ships
FontAwesome 6 contact icons (replacing the emoji icons of
an earlier iteration).

**Persistence + product stack.**
- **Neon serverless Postgres** with **Drizzle ORM** for
  user resumes, generation history, and credit ledgers
- **Upstash Redis** for short-lived cache + rate-control
  state
- **Stack Auth** for the authentication surface
- **Stripe** for subscription billing and the credit system
- **Cloudinary** for user uploads and rendered-PDF storage
- **nanoid** for the shareable-token URL scheme

**UI.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The
**Neobrutalism design system** (hard shadows, thick borders,
high contrast) is the deliberate visual identity — chosen
and refined in the Phase 3 UI/UX overhaul branded
internally as *"sell results, not tools"*. shadcn/ui +
Tailwind for primitives; Framer Motion for transitions;
react-hook-form + Zod resolvers for input validation.

**Deployment — three migrations, one production.** Started
on **Railway**, migrated to **Cloudflare Workers**
(2026-02-22), then migrated again to a **DigitalOcean VPS**
(2026-04-06) because the Typst binary needs a real
filesystem and a long-lived container. Production now runs
as a single **Docker container** on the VPS behind
**Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), continuously
deployed by **GitHub Actions** on every push to the
deployment branch. The Dockerfile uses `curl` (not `wget`)
and `xz-utils` for the Typst tarball — both fixes captured
in the commit log as real edge-case failures during the
VPS migration.
narrative.outcomes:
Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users
with the full free + paid credit flow. **168 commits over
~3 months** (2026-02-21 → 2026-05-09), **~95% Chan-authored**
across two GitHub identities (8 commits from Claude
co-author, 1 from Vercel deploy bot, rest solo). Three
deployment migrations and a LaTeX → Typst document-engine
swap shipped without service outage — every migration step
is in the commit log including the specific Docker
fixes (`xz-utils`, `curl`, FontAwesome package pre-cache),
the strict-JSON-schema regression diagnosis, and the
`sessionStorage` migration for sensitive input. Mobile
reliability + responsive UI pass shipped 2026-05-09 after
the production hardening phase.
narrative.myRole:
Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed
the 7-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages
(migrating the editor from CopilotKit to Vercel AI SDK
agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built
the Stripe credit system, configured Stack Auth, designed
the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens,
executed all three deployment migrations (Railway → CF
Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst
engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes for `xz-utils`
and the Typst FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the
Neobrutalism UI in the "sell results, not tools" Phase 3
overhaul, built the natural-language refinement loop in
the Editor, and continues to operate the production
service as recently as the May 2026 mobile reliability
pass.

projects.name: FanFic Lab — HSR DreamWriter, an Adaptive LangGraph Agent Powering a Gaming Fan-Creation Community
projects.tagline: A full-stack AI co-creation platform for the *Honkai: Star Rail* (崩坏：星穹铁道) fandom — a 7-node LangGraph adaptive agent (intent parser → architect → writer → quality guard → revision loop → summarizer → delivery) with a self-revising quality loop, hand-curated 5-character HSR canon knowledge pack + RAG over a `KnowledgeChunk` pgvector index, and a UGC community layer with follows / nested-reply comments / notifications / pgvector related-story recommendations — split into two Docker services (`web` + `agent`) self-hosted on a DigitalOcean VPS via Coolify behind Cloudflare.
techStack: Radix UI primitives (Dialog, DropdownMenu, Popover, ScrollArea, Tabs, Avatar, Checkbox, Collapsible, Progress, Select, Separator, Slot)
Motion (formerly Framer Motion) 12 — parallax hero, transitions
LangGraph.js 1.0 + @langchain/core 1.1 + @langchain/openai 1.2 + @langchain/langgraph-cli 1.1 — runs as a separate Docker service on port 8123
OpenAI SDK 6.33 — GPT-4o (creative writer, temp 0.9) + GPT-4o-mini (intent parser, quality guard at temp 0.3) + text-embedding-3-small (1536-dim vectors)
Tavily Search SDK (@tavily/core 0.6 + @langchain/tavily 1.2) — web research for non-canon fandoms
Prisma 7.2 + @prisma/adapter-pg 7.2 + Neon serverless 1.0 — type-safe ORM on Neon PostgreSQL
pgvector + ivfflat cosine index (`Story.embedding vector(1536)`, lists=100) — related-story recommendations
pg_trgm GIN indexes (`Story_title_trgm_idx`, `Story_summary_trgm_idx`) — fast ILIKE substring search
KnowledgeChunk table with `vector(1536)` embeddings + RAG retrieval helper (`retrieveRelevantChunks`) feeding the writer node
Redis (ioredis 5.8) — Tavily research cache with 30-day TTL
Stack Auth 2.8 — managed authentication; user rows synced to Postgres on first sign-in
Cloudinary 2.8 — story covers + avatars (with react-easy-crop 5.5 client-side crop preview)
Zod 3.25 — runtime validation of agent state, request payloads, knowledge schemas
Sonner 2.0 — toast notifications · next-themes 0.4 — dark mode
Docker Compose two-service deployment (`Dockerfile.web` + `Dockerfile.agent`) on a DigitalOcean VPS
Traefik reverse proxy (built into Coolify) routing :80/:443 to web + agent containers
Cloudflare proxy mode — SSL termination + CDN + DDoS protection
GitHub Actions CI: build → push to GHCR (`ghcr.io/chanmeng666/fanfic-lab/{web,agent}`) → SSH-deploy to VPS on `git push master`
keywords: Adaptive LangGraph agent with quality-loop revision
Honkai: Star Rail fandom-specific knowledge pack
pgvector RAG over hand-curated lore chunks

projects.publicSummary: FanFic Lab (live at https://fanfic-lab.tech) is a full-stack AI co-creation community built specifically for the *Honkai: Star Rail* (崩坏：星穹铁道) fandom — the most popular HoYoverse game in China during the project window. The 182-commit codebase compresses three usually-separate things into one product: (1) **an adaptive LangGraph.js agent named "DreamWriter"** that takes a Chinese natural-language prompt like "想看砂金和藿藿的现代AU虐转甜" and runs it through a 7-node graph (intent parser → story architect → writer → quality guard → conditional revision loop → summarizer → delivery), where the quality-guard node uses GPT-4o-mini to score the draft 1-10 against an HSR canon knowledge prompt and either passes the story through or sends it back to the writer for up to MAX_REVISIONS=2 self-corrections; (2) **a hand-curated fandom knowledge pack** at `src/knowledge/hsr/` containing 5 deeply-modeled HSR characters (砂金 / 藿藿 / 丹恒 / 景元 / 花火) with personality / speech-pattern / emotional-trigger / relationship maps + 4 relationship dynamics + 5 world-rule categories (命途体系, etc.) + 5 trope templates (现代AU, ABO, Hurt/Comfort, 豆花/甜饼, 前世今生/命运轮回), composed into a system prompt by a `toSystemPrompt()` method and combined at runtime with **RAG over a `KnowledgeChunk` pgvector table** (1536-dim `text-embedding-3-small` vectors, retrieved by the writer node before drafting); and (3) **a UGC community layer** with Stack-Auth identities, public follows, public author pages, nested-reply comments + comment likes + spam protection, in-app notifications with a bell + page, per-reader font/line-height/scroll-position preferences, dark mode, per-story view tracking, pgvector-cosine related-story recommendations, pg_trgm GIN-indexed search, and SEO/OG tags + sitemap. The whole thing is split into **two Docker services** — `web` (Next.js 16 frontend + API routes) and `agent` (LangGraph CLI runtime on port 8123) — wired together via Docker Compose internal networking, self-hosted on a **DigitalOcean VPS via Coolify** behind Cloudflare proxy-mode SSL, with GitHub Actions building both images to GHCR and SSH-deploying on every `git push master`. The migration from Railway to DigitalOcean+Coolify (commit `734e69c`, 2026-04-03) is documented in a 600+-line `docs/MIGRATION_RAILWAY_TO_DIGITALOCEAN.md` playbook that doubles as a reusable Claude-Code automation reference. Before the HSR DreamWriter pivot in commit `a288e25` (2026-03-23), the project shipped as a more generic CopilotKit + LangGraph fandom-agnostic editor with HITL approval cards, Tavily fandom research, and a creative wizard — that earlier work survives in `docs/COPILOTKIT_LANGGRAPH_HITL_GUIDE.md` and informed the current agent design. The pivot commit `b5baf34` the same day deliberately removed the old editor/wizard/generator code, demonstrating willingness to delete working code when a sharper product direction emerges.

narrative.problem: The Chinese gaming fanfiction (同人文) scene around HoYoverse titles — and *Honkai: Star Rail* / 崩坏：星穹铁道 in particular — has two structural problems that generic AI writing tools don't solve. **First**, generic LLM creative writing assistants (CopilotKit-style autosuggest, ChatGPT, Claude) have no deep grounding in HSR canon: they default to surface-level character clichés ("cold prince" / "shy boy"), get the speech patterns and emotional triggers wrong (e.g., they don't know that 砂金 stops speaking softly when 黑天鹅 is threatened, or that 藿藿's stutter is canon-specific), and routinely produce OOC (out-of-character) drafts that any serious fanwriter rejects. **Second**, existing fanfiction platforms (LOFTER / AO3 / 长佩) are *reading* communities, not *creation* communities — they host finished work, but they don't lower the activation energy for the much larger group of fans who want to consume CP (couple/ship) content but find a blank page intimidating. The opportunity: build a platform where any fan can type "I want to read 砂金 × 藿藿 现代AU 虐转甜 HE" in Chinese, get back a canon-respecting 3000-word draft in 60 seconds, edit it, publish it, and have other fans like/comment/follow them. That requires solving the OOC problem at the agent level (canon knowledge baked in, plus a self-revising quality loop) and the activation-energy problem at the product level (one-shot prompt → finished story → save → publish). FanFic Lab is the answer.
narrative.audience: Three concentric circles. **Innermost**: the 同人圈 (fandom writers' circle) for *Honkai: Star Rail* — typically Chinese-speaking female fans of HoYoverse games who lurk on LOFTER / Weibo / Bilibili, who already know who 砂金 / 藿藿 / 丹恒 are, who have CP preferences and trope preferences (现代AU / ABO / Hurt-Comfort), and who want a co-writer that won't break their characters. **Middle ring**: consumers who like reading HSR fic but never wrote any — they get the one-prompt-to-finished-story path and become first-time creators, then liking/commenting on others' work pulls them into the community. **Outer ring**: the broader gaming-fanfic and CN-creative-writing community who can adopt the same architecture for other fandoms (the `src/knowledge/` directory is deliberately structured as `base/types.ts` + per-fandom packs, with HSR as the only currently-shipped pack but mentor / 原神 / 鸣潮 packs as natural future expansions — `FandomKnowledge` is a typed interface, not an HSR-hardcode). The Chinese-first product surface is a deliberate choice: prompts, system messages, character data, world rules, even the agent's internal log messages ("正在根据反馈修改第 X 版...") are all in Chinese, because the audience is.
narrative.productLogic: The user flow collapses what would normally be 4-5 separate apps (writing assistant + character database + fanfic editor + social platform + recommendation engine) into one screen. (1) **Land on `https://fanfic-lab.tech`** — cinematic full-bleed hero with Honkai: Star Rail splash arts and KV (key-visual) imagery, plus a Honkai: Star Rail fan content attribution credit (the project takes the IP-attribution discipline seriously even though it's a non-commercial fan tool). (2) **Sign in via Stack Auth** — branded sign-in/up shell with explicit error classification, mobile + perf polish. (3) **Hit the "create" page** — type a Chinese prompt describing the CP you want to read about ("想看砂金和藿藿的现代AU虐转甜"). (4) **Backend kicks off a DreamWriter agent run**: the Next.js API route POSTs to `LANGGRAPH_URL=http://agent:8123` (Docker Compose internal hostname) which runs the 7-node graph; the frontend polls for `logs` updates and shows them in Chinese ("正在解析您的需求..." → "正在策划故事..." → "正在创作..." → "正在质量检查 (8/10)..." → "正在根据反馈修改第 1 版..." → "故事完成"). (5) **Story auto-publishes to the Fandom Feed** (a deliberate product decision in commit `b090c04` "auto-publish stories, remove shelf page and drafts tab" — friction kills first-time creators). (6) **Other users discover it via**: the feed (filterable by fandom / ships / tags / rating / status, sortable by recent / popular / comments / word count, with infinite scroll); SEO/OG tags + sitemap for organic search; **pgvector related-story recommendations** on each story page (commit `48428e6` — cosine similarity over title+summary+ships+tags embeddings, deliberately *not* over chapter content because we want thematic matching, not prose-style matching); **per-author public pages** at `/users/[username]` with their stories + followers + following. (7) **Engagement primitives**: like a story, follow an author, leave a nested-reply comment, like comments, get notified in the bell + notifications page when someone interacts with your work. (8) **Story reader page**: per-reader font / line-height preferences (commit `6551966`) + scroll position memory so the reader can pause + resume, plus dark mode toggle in the header (commit `b922cf8`). (9) **Author tools**: edit published stories, continue any story with a new chapter (with auto-generated chapter titles, commit `30df8fe`), avatar crop preview via react-easy-crop. The cumulative effect: **a fan-creation community where the friction from "I want to read this" to "this story exists and other fans are commenting on it" is one Chinese sentence**.
narrative.businessLogic: Zero monetisation in the current iteration — this is an MIT-licensed open-source fan-creation tool with a non-commercial usage stance toward the HSR IP (it ships an explicit Honkai: Star Rail fan content attribution on the homepage and treats the canon knowledge pack as a *fan-curated* reference, not commercial use of HoYoverse assets). The infrastructure economics are however explicit and deliberate. **Cost model**: the production stack runs on a **single $12/month DigitalOcean droplet** (the Coolify migration playbook records the choice in detail — Railway was costing ~$10/month and getting more expensive as the agent service scaled, so the move to a self-hosted droplet behind Coolify trades a small monthly delta for full control over runtime + the ability to host more services on the same box). **$80 of DigitalOcean credits** fund ~6.5 months of runway. **OpenAI cost containment**: GPT-4o-mini (cheaper) handles the intent parser + quality guard nodes, GPT-4o (more expensive) only handles the writer node where prose quality matters; the quality-guard revision budget is capped at MAX_REVISIONS=2 to prevent runaway cost on a single story; embeddings use `text-embedding-3-small` (cheaper than `text-embedding-3-large`); the `SourceResearchCache` Prisma model + Redis tier persist Tavily results for 30 days to avoid re-paying for the same fandom research. **Credits scaffolding**: the Prisma schema includes `UserCredits` and `Generation` models with `creditsCharged: Int` — credits infrastructure exists in the data layer (foundation for a future per-generation pricing model) but is not yet enforced in production, so the current version is fully free for users. **Build-time + runtime layout**: two services (web + agent) in one Docker Compose file means health checks, internal networking, restart policies, and rolling deploys are all handled by one Coolify project rather than fragmented across providers. **Discovery loop**: per-story SEO/OG tags + sitemap (commit `897a43d`) + the related-stories pgvector recommender are designed to compound organic discoverability over time without an ad budget.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The system is organised around four orthogonal layers that together produce the user-visible behaviour.

**Layer 1 — Adaptive LangGraph agent with self-revising quality loop.** `src/agent/dreamwriter/graph.ts` defines a `StateGraph<DreamWriterStateAnnotation>` with 7 nodes wired into a deliberate topology: **intent_parser** (GPT-4o-mini, temp 0.3, extracts `{cp, setting, tone, constraints, language}` from Chinese natural-language input) → **story_architect** (GPT-4o, plans `{title, cp, setting, tone, wordTarget, emotionalArc, scenes[]}`) → **writer** (GPT-4o, temp 0.9, generates prose using the HSR knowledge prompt + RAG context) → **quality_guard** (GPT-4o-mini, temp 0.3, scores the draft against the same knowledge prompt and returns `{overallScore, oocIssues[], consistencyIssues[], proseNotes[], passesThreshold}`) → **conditional edge** `routeAfterQualityCheck`: if `!passesThreshold && revisionCount < MAX_REVISIONS(=2)`, route back through **revision_counter** → **writer** with the quality feedback appended to the prompt; otherwise → **summarize** (writes the public-facing one-paragraph blurb) → **delivery** (END). The state annotation includes a `logs[]` reducer so the frontend can show Chinese progress text in near-real-time. `MemorySaver` checkpointing is used so runs survive process restarts during long agent calls.

**Layer 2 — Hand-curated fandom knowledge pack + pgvector RAG.** Two complementary mechanisms feed the writer node: **(a) the deterministic knowledge prompt** in `src/knowledge/hsr/` — `characters.ts` defines 5 HSR characters (砂金 / 藿藿 / 丹恒 / 景元 / 花火) as `CharacterProfile` objects with `name`, `aliases[]`, `personality[]`, `speechPatterns`, `emotionalTriggers[]`, `relationships: Record<string, string>`, and `timelineStates`; `relationships.ts` has 4 `RelationshipDynamic` objects with `keyMoments[]`; `world.ts` has 5 `WorldRule` categories (命途体系 etc.); `tropes.ts` has 5 `TropeTemplate` objects (现代AU / ABO / Hurt-Comfort / 豆花/甜饼 / 前世今生) with per-character `characterAdaptations`; `index.ts` composes everything via a `toSystemPrompt()` method that produces a structured Chinese prompt with `▎character` headers + `性格特征` / `说话方式` / `情感触发点` / `人际关系` / `时间线状态` fields. **(b) The probabilistic RAG layer** in `src/knowledge/base/rag.ts` — `retrieveRelevantChunks(query, fandom, limit)` embeds the query via `text-embedding-3-small`, runs a raw SQL `embedding <=> vector` cosine-distance query against the `KnowledgeChunk` table (with a pgvector ivfflat index), returns the top-k chunks. The writer node calls both, concatenating the deterministic knowledge prompt with the RAG context before invoking GPT-4o. This split lets the *fixed* canon (character personalities) be edited as code while the *long-tail* canon (specific scenes, story snippets) lives as embeddings and grows over time.

**Layer 3 — Two-Docker-service production deployment via DigitalOcean + Coolify.** The codebase ships **two Dockerfiles** (`Dockerfile.web` for Next.js 16 + Prisma 7, `Dockerfile.agent` for LangGraph CLI) and one `docker-compose.coolify.yml` that wires them into a single Coolify project. Internal networking uses Docker Compose service names (`web` calls `http://agent:8123` via the Compose-managed bridge network — no Railway-internal-DNS, no nginx, no manual sidecar config). **Health checks** are explicit: `web` polls `/api/health` (which itself checks Redis + Postgres latency), `agent` polls LangGraph's `/info` endpoint, both with `start_period: 30-40s` to tolerate cold starts. **External proxy chain**: Cloudflare (proxy mode, SSL termination, CDN) → DigitalOcean droplet 159.223.173.17 → Traefik (built into Coolify, handles `:80`/`:443` and routes by host) → Docker container. **CI/CD**: `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` on every `git push master` runs a 2-image parallel Docker Buildx build, pushes both to GHCR (`ghcr.io/chanmeng666/fanfic-lab/{web,agent}` tagged per-SHA + `latest`, with separate `cache-from` scopes for each image), then SSH-deploys to the VPS with a 20-minute timeout. The 600+ line `docs/MIGRATION_RAILWAY_TO_DIGITALOCEAN.md` playbook documents the entire transition including DNS cutover, Cloudflare config, Coolify project setup, lessons learned (Docker registry case-sensitivity bug fixed in commit `839e9c1`), and a "Claude Code Automation Playbook" section — turning the migration into a reusable artifact.

**Layer 4 — Full UGC community substrate with pgvector recommendations + trigram search.** The Prisma schema models 21 tables centred on `User` ↔ `Story` ↔ `Chapter` with full social graph (`Follow`), engagement primitives (`Like`, `Comment` with `parentId` self-reference for nested replies, `CommentLike`), discovery primitives (`Notification` with polymorphic `payload: Json`), preferences (`UserPreferences` includes `preferredCPs[]`, `preferredTropes[]`, `avoidTropes[]`, `tonePreference`, `lengthPreference` — feeding into the personalised feed *and* into future agent-prompt personalisation), and the AI-generation audit trail (`Generation` model with `request: Json`, `plan: Json`, `deliverable: Json`, `tokensUsed`, `creditsCharged`, `durationMs`). **Two specialised PostgreSQL extensions** are deliberately exploited: **(i) pg_trgm** with GIN indexes on `Story.title` and `Story.summary` (migration `20260421091500_enable_pgtrgm_search`) — turns Prisma's `contains:` queries from sequential scans into trigram-index lookups, scaling search past a few hundred stories without code change; **(ii) pgvector** with an ivfflat cosine index on `Story.embedding` (migration `20260421093000_add_story_embedding`, `lists=100`) — every story gets a 1536-dim embedding generated from `title + fandom + ships + tags + summary` (deliberately *not* chapter content, because we want thematic match, not prose-style match), and the related-stories endpoint runs cosine-distance queries to surface similar work. `setStoryEmbedding()` in `lib/story-embedding.ts` uses raw SQL because Prisma 7 doesn't yet support direct writes to the `vector` type. A `scripts/backfill-embeddings.ts` script handles legacy stories created before the embedding column existed.

The four layers are loosely coupled: the agent can be re-shaped without touching the DB schema, the knowledge pack can grow without touching the agent topology, the deployment substrate can move providers (the Railway → DigitalOcean migration is the proof) without touching the application, and the community layer's pgvector + pg_trgm indexes are content-agnostic.
narrative.outcomes:
- **182 commits over ~16 weeks** (2026-01-01 → 2026-04-21), spanning a complete product redesign mid-project: the first iteration was a generic CopilotKit + LangGraph fanfic editor with HITL approval cards; the HSR DreamWriter pivot in commit `a288e25` ("HSR DreamWriter - complete product redesign", 2026-03-23) plus commit `b5baf34` ("remove old pipeline, editor, wizard, and generator code", same day) deliberately deleted the old codebase to focus on a single fandom with deep canon support.
- **Adaptive quality-loop agent shipped** in commit `a06d96f` "wire up adaptive DreamWriter agent graph with quality loop" — the conditional revision edge with MAX_REVISIONS=2 turns a one-shot generator into a self-correcting agent, dramatically reducing OOC errors per Chan's internal observations.
- **Hand-curated HSR canon knowledge pack** built across commits `e5e82ac` (initial pack) → `1a0fa53` (HSR_TROPES extraction) → `611d0e0` (KnowledgeChunk model + DreamWriter preference fields in `UserPreferences`) → `f9b1fcf` (RAG utilities). 5 deeply-modeled characters + 4 relationships + 5 world rules + 5 trope templates.
- **Railway → DigitalOcean + Coolify migration** completed 2026-04-03 (commit `734e69c`), documented in a 600+-line reusable playbook at `docs/MIGRATION_RAILWAY_TO_DIGITALOCEAN.md` that includes lessons-learned (Docker registry case-sensitivity, SSH timeout tuning) and a "Claude Code Automation Playbook" section.
- **GitHub Actions zero-downtime CI/CD** added in commit `985e0b4` "ci: add GitHub Actions CI/CD for zero-downtime deployment" — parallel image builds with per-image cache scopes, GHCR registry, SSH-deployment-by-push.
- **Full social graph + discovery substrate** built in the last 4 days of active development (2026-04-21): nested-reply comments + likes + spam protection (`d234471`), in-app notifications (`f171ce3`), public followers/following pages (`d96fc68`), per-author public pages (`e465240`), pgvector related-stories (`48428e6`), pg_trgm search indexes (`9bd86a9`), per-story view counts (`f598394`), per-story SEO/OG + sitemap (`897a43d`), dark mode (`b922cf8`), reader font/line-height + scroll memory (`6551966`), avatar crop preview (`9554785`), branded sign-in/up shell (`a8fccd3`).
- **Production stack survives a multi-service migration**: the same codebase ran on Vercel + Railway, then Vercel + Railway-LangGraph-only, then DigitalOcean + Coolify (current), without breaking the agent contract.
- **Cost discipline**: 2 services running on $12/month DO droplet; $80 of credits = ~6.5 months free; LLM cost containment via model tiering (mini for parsing/checking, full for prose) + cache-first Tavily research + capped revision budget.
- **Production URL live**: https://fanfic-lab.tech/ (with www → root redirect), Cloudflare-proxied, MIT-licensed.
narrative.myRole: Sole author, designer, and operator. Every one of the 182 commits is mine. I wrote both the initial CopilotKit + LangGraph fanfic editor (commits `0a3d1f5` → `36c5469` Teal+Amber "Literary Atelier" design system → `cdc3586` Railway deployment), made the call to scrap that direction and pivot to HSR-specific DreamWriter (commits `cbd31cc` "pivot from MDZS to Honkai: Star Rail based on 2025-2026 fandom trends" → `4790851` MDZS implementation plan as comparison reference → `a288e25` "HSR DreamWriter - complete product redesign" + `b5baf34` the same-day old-code deletion), hand-curated the entire HSR canon knowledge pack (5 characters with their specific 性格特征 / 说话方式 / 情感触发点 — this is bespoke fandom-domain knowledge, not scraped), wrote the 7-node adaptive LangGraph topology including the conditional revision edge, designed the Prisma schema (21 models including the polymorphic `Notification.payload: Json`, the self-referential `Comment.parentId` for nested replies, the deliberate split between `KnowledgeChunk` for RAG and `SourceResearchCache` for Tavily caching, the `UserCredits` + `Generation` audit foundation), wrote both Dockerfiles + the Coolify compose file + the GitHub Actions deploy workflow, executed the Railway → DigitalOcean + Coolify migration (and *documented* it as a reusable playbook), wrote the pgvector + pg_trgm migrations and the `lib/story-embedding.ts` raw-SQL escape hatch, and built the entire community-feature sprint (comments, notifications, follows, related stories, search indexing, view counts, SEO, dark mode, reader preferences, avatar cropping) in the final ~96-hour push 2026-04-18 → 2026-04-21. The project doubles as a personal R&D lab for the rest of the portfolio: the Coolify-on-DO migration playbook informed similar deployments in archcanvas (Workers → DO VPS), the GitHub-Actions-to-GHCR-to-SSH pattern reappeared in subsequent multi-service projects, and the pgvector + ivfflat + cosine-similarity recipe became the template I reused for related-content recommendations elsewhere.

projects.name: AI Image Generator — Full-Stack Text-to-Image SaaS

Two architectural pivots (Vercel->CF Workers free-AI 2026-04-09, then full SaaS layer one day later) turned a 2024 demo into a production product.
